06 · Bonus — heatmaps, correlation & a PCA ordination

Trends in multi-omics data analysis for Microbial Ecology and Biotechnology — UFZ Leipzig

Instructor: Anderson Santos

R refresher session

Learning objectives (time-buffer lesson)

Use this if the core session finishes early. You will:

  • Draw an abundance heatmap of top genera with geom_tile().
  • Compute and visualise a correlation structure.
  • Run a PCA ordination with base R (prcomp) — no Bioconductor.

All CRAN-only. Nothing here is new machinery — just new combinations.

0 · Setup

library(readr); library(dplyr); library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2); library(forcats); library(tibble)

data_dir <- if (file.exists("../data/sample_metadata.csv")) "../data" else "data"
meta     <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "sample_metadata.csv"))
taxonomy <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "taxonomy.csv"))
abund    <- read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "genus_abundance.csv"))

ab_long <- abund |>
  pivot_longer(-sample_id, names_to = "genus", values_to = "abundance") |>
  left_join(taxonomy, by = "genus")

1 · A heatmap of the most abundant genera

Idea: pick the 20 most abundant genera overall, average their relative abundance per region, and draw a tile grid — colour = abundance.

# Top 20 genera by total abundance across the study.
top_genera <- ab_long |>
  group_by(genus) |>
  summarise(total = sum(abundance)) |>
  slice_max(total, n = 20) |>
  pull(genus)

# Mean relative abundance of those genera per region.
heat <- ab_long |>
  filter(genus %in% top_genera) |>
  left_join(select(meta, sample_id, nationality), by = "sample_id") |>
  group_by(sample_id) |>
  mutate(rel = abundance / sum(abundance)) |>
  group_by(nationality, genus) |>
  summarise(mean_rel = mean(rel), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(heat, aes(x = nationality,
                 y = fct_reorder(genus, mean_rel, sum),
                 fill = mean_rel)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "C", labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Top 20 genera across regions",
       x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Mean\nrel. abund.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))

📌 Teaching note. viridis palettes are perceptually uniform and colour-blind-friendly — a good default for heatmaps. scale_fill_viridis_c() ships with ggplot2, no extra package needed.

2 · Correlation between the major phyla

Do phyla trade off against each other across samples? Build a sample × phylum relative-abundance matrix and correlate the columns.

phy_wide <- ab_long |>
  group_by(sample_id, phylum) |>
  summarise(a = sum(abundance), .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(sample_id) |>
  mutate(rel = a / sum(a)) |>
  ungroup() |>                          # important: drop the sample_id grouping
  select(sample_id, phylum, rel) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = phylum, values_from = rel, values_fill = 0)

cmat <- cor(as.matrix(select(phy_wide, -sample_id)))
round(cmat, 2)
#>                 Actinobacteria Bacteroidetes Cyanobacteria Firmicutes
#> Actinobacteria            1.00         -0.32         -0.08       0.16
#> Bacteroidetes            -0.32          1.00          0.05      -0.98
#> Cyanobacteria            -0.08          0.05          1.00      -0.04
#> Firmicutes                0.16         -0.98         -0.04       1.00
#> Fusobacteria              0.15         -0.21         -0.01       0.18
#> Proteobacteria           -0.01          0.00          0.01      -0.13
#> Spirochaetes              0.09         -0.04         -0.04       0.01
#> Verrucomicrobia           0.08         -0.16          0.05       0.10
#>                 Fusobacteria Proteobacteria Spirochaetes Verrucomicrobia
#> Actinobacteria          0.15          -0.01         0.09            0.08
#> Bacteroidetes          -0.21           0.00        -0.04           -0.16
#> Cyanobacteria          -0.01           0.01        -0.04            0.05
#> Firmicutes              0.18          -0.13         0.01            0.10
#> Fusobacteria            1.00           0.07         0.30            0.02
#> Proteobacteria          0.07           1.00         0.09            0.06
#> Spirochaetes            0.30           0.09         1.00            0.01
#> Verrucomicrobia         0.02           0.06         0.01            1.00

# Visualise the correlation matrix as a heatmap.
as.data.frame(cmat) |>
  rownames_to_column("phylum1") |>
  pivot_longer(-phylum1, names_to = "phylum2", values_to = "r") |>
  ggplot(aes(phylum1, phylum2, fill = r)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "steelblue", mid = "white", high = "firebrick",
                       midpoint = 0, limits = c(-1, 1)) +
  labs(title = "Correlation between phyla (relative abundance)",
       x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "r") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))

The classic gut signal often appears here: Firmicutes and Bacteroidetes correlate negatively — as one rises, the other tends to fall.

3 · PCA ordination (base R prcomp)

Principal Component Analysis summarises many correlated variables into a few axes. We ordinate samples by their phylum profile and colour by region.

mat <- phy_wide |> column_to_rownames("sample_id") |> as.matrix()

pca <- prcomp(mat, scale. = TRUE)      # PCA on the phylum relative abundances
var_expl <- round(100 * pca$sdev^2 / sum(pca$sdev^2), 1)   # % variance per axis

scores <- as.data.frame(pca$x[, 1:2]) |>
  rownames_to_column("sample_id") |>
  left_join(select(meta, sample_id, nationality), by = "sample_id")

ggplot(scores, aes(PC1, PC2, colour = nationality)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1.6) +
  stat_ellipse(level = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "PCA of gut communities (phylum level)",
       x = paste0("PC1 (", var_expl[1], "%)"),
       y = paste0("PC2 (", var_expl[2], "%)"),
       colour = "Region") +
  theme_minimal()

📌 Teaching note. PCA (base R) and NMDS (from the capstone, via vegan) are both ordinations. PCA is fast and linear; NMDS handles ecological (Bray–Curtis) distances better. Showing both makes the concept stick.

Recap

  • geom_tile() turns any summarised long table into a heatmap.
  • cor() + a diverging fill scale reveals relationships between variables.
  • prcomp() gives a quick PCA ordination — all base R + ggplot2, CRAN-only.

That closes the R refresher. From here, everything in the week is more of the same verbs and plots on richer data. Well done!
